items.q: When solving 3x = 12, what do you do to both sides?
items.correct: Divide both sides by 3 to get x = 4.
items.misconception: Subtract 3 from both sides because 3 was multiplying x.
items.random_wrong: Multiply both sides by 3 to undo the equation.
items.why: Students confuse inverse operations — they remember 'do the opposite' but mix up that the opposite of multiplication is division, not subtraction.

items.q: If the discriminant of a quadratic is negative, what does it tell you about real roots?
items.correct: There are no real roots — the graph does not cross the x-axis.
items.misconception: There is one repeated root — the curve just touches the x-axis.
items.why: Students confuse 'discriminant zero' (one repeated root) with 'discriminant negative' (no real roots) because both feel like 'unusual' cases.

items.q: Which average is best to use for the data set [2, 3, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 100]?
items.correct: The median (3.5) — it isn't distorted by the outlier 100, unlike the mean.
items.misconception: The mean — it always gives the most accurate average.
items.random_wrong: The mode, because the most common value is always the best representative.
items.why: Students treat 'the mean' as the default for every data set, without considering whether outliers make a different measure of centre more appropriate.
Correct answer: x = 4. “x = 8” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's 3. “e³” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The real figure is 100. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
